optimist.name: Gretchen Hartmann
optimist.column:
The Cardinals lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour the mood. You think an eight-game skid matters? Honey, the Cardinals invented the comeback. Molina could un-retire tomorrow and I’d believe it. This team’s got a heartbeat deeper than a St. Louis winter, and it’s still ticking.  

Let’s talk about that 2011 Series, shall we? The way they clawed their way back from 3-1 down, the way the crowd at Busch Stadium turned into a cathedral of hope. That’s the magic we’re talking about—devil magic, baby. Not the kind that bends

pessimist.name: Vern Kratzinger
pessimist.column:
The Cardinals lost again. Not to a team that’s been dominant, not to a team that’s been rebuilding, but to the Boston Red Sox, a team that’s been… well, Boston. Seven runs. One run. A performance that made you wonder if the 2023 Cardinals are a team that plays baseball or a team that plays… something else. Not the Cardinal Way.  

Mozeliak has mistaken a rebuild for a vacation. This isn’t Redbird baseball. This is the ghost of a better franchise wearing the same laundry. The front office’s latest experiment—another manager named Mike—hasn’t just failed; it’s become a punchline. Mike Matheny, Mike Shildt, Mike Borzello. Each one a fresh coat of paint on a rotting canvas. And now, Mike Mathis? No, not that Mike. The guy who’s been here since the last rebuild. The guy who’s been here since the last time the Cardinals tried to be competitive.  

The problem isn’t just the managers. It’s the philosophy. The Cardinals used to be a team that played with grit, with identity. They had a way of making you believe in the underdog, even when they weren’t. Now? They’re a team that looks lost, a team that seems to have forgotten how to win.
